cue: How do you say you're in serious trouble?

es.text: Estar con el agua al cuello
es.literal: to be with the water at the neck
es.meaning: To be in serious trouble, usually financial.
es.example: Con esa deuda estamos con el agua al cuello.
es.exampleTranslation: With that debt we're in over our heads.

it.literal: to have the water at the throat
it.meaning: To be in serious difficulty, often from money or a deadline.
it.example: Con questa scadenza ho l'acqua alla gola.

bridge.note: The same rising water, stopped one inch apart: Spanish measures it at the NECK, Italian at the THROAT — outside versus inside, the part you'd grab versus the part you'd breathe through. And the verbs part company: Spanish is WITH the water (estar con), Italian HAS it (avere), which is the same have/be split that catches learners everywhere else in these two languages.

interference.es_to_it: 'Essere con l'acqua al collo' half-works but the fixed form is avere l'acqua ALLA GOLA — have, not be, and throat, not neck.
interference.it_to_es: 'Tener el agua a la garganta' is not the Spanish idiom: estar CON el agua AL CUELLO.

review.notes: Discovered via an Instagram card set Joe shared 2026-07-20 (discovery only; all glosses and examples authored fresh). ES counterpart chosen by Claude from recall — UNVERIFIED. Best have/be trap in the bank after 0080; the neck/throat inch is the delight.
